statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
[Bowser's Castle — Dani Donadi, Super Nintendo World: New Super Mario Bros. Wii ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=me0AJ5bIxCM) ![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2207H1/24370cbd2b4d81913e291b03a0c40c2108851dcc71772bb33e4c204da2e5748b.png)This is the easy version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that in this version, $ n \leq 70 $ and you may make $ 10^4 $ queries. You can hack only if you solved all versions of this problem.

This is an interactive problem.

Let $ n $ be a positive integer. A function on $ n $ variables $ x_1, \ldots, x_n $ is called min-max if it can be constructed as writing only $ \min $ and $ \max $ calls around $ x_1, \ldots, x_n $ with each variable appearing exactly once in order from left to right in the expression. For example, $ \min(x_1, x_2, x_3) $ is a min-max function on $ 3 $ variables, but $ \max(\min(x_1, x_3), x_2) $ and $ \min(\max(x_1, x_2), \max(x_2, x_3)) $ are not.

Bowser has chosen a min-max function on $ n $ ( $ 2 \leq n \leq 70 $ ) variables and tells you $ n $ . Additionally, he lets you ask queries of the following form: you give Bowser $ n $ integers $ x_1, \ldots, x_n $ ( $ 1 \leq x_i \leq 10^9 $ ) as input, and he tells you $ f(x_1, \ldots, x_n) $ .

To escape his castle, you have to deduce his function using at most $ 10^4 $ queries in total. After that, to prove to him that you have learned the function, Bowser will give you up to $ 5000 $ of his own inputs $ x_1, \ldots, x_n $ , to which you must respond with the correct value of $ f(x_1, \ldots, x_n) $ .

Since Bowser's castle is very secure, he actually has multiple functions for you to figure out, with a total variable count of at most $ 70 $ . Additionally, he constrains that you use at most $ 10^4 $ queries across all of the functions and that he will also not ask more than $ 5000 $ queries in total.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 35 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 2 \leq n \leq 70 $ ) — the number of variables in the min-max function.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 70 $ .

After you read this line of input, the interaction begins with your first query.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
2
3

5

2

3 6 2

3 5 7

0
4

8

3

8 7 6 5

0
Output
? 5 4 3

? 1 2 1

!

6

7

? 1 9 8 7

? 5 1000000000 2 3

!

6

Note
In the first test case, Bowser's min-max function is $ f := \max(x_1, x_2, x_3) $ . After asking two queries to receive $ f(5, 4, 3) = 5 $ and $ f(1, 2, 1) = 2 $ from the jury, the solution is ready to answer the jury's queries. It correctly deduces that $ f(3, 6, 2) = 6 $ and $ f(3, 5, 7) = 7 $ .

In the second test case, Bowser's min-max function is $ f := \min(\max(x_1, x_2), \max(x_3, x_4)) $ . After asking two queries to receive $ f(1, 9, 8, 7) = 8 $ and $ f(5, 10^9, 2, 3) = 3 $ from the jury, the solution is ready to answer the jury's queries. It correctly deduces that $ f(8, 7, 6, 5) = 6 $ .

Note that it is not guaranteed that the queries in the example uniquely determine the min-max function. The example is there only to demonstrate how the queries work. Empty lines in the example input and output are given only for better readability; you don't need to output them in your solution.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Use 0-1 sorting to make the structure a little simpler. What's a concise way to visualize a min max function?

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: The min max function is a tree that alternates min/max across depths. Can you determine the type of the top operation?

hints.title: Hint 3
hints.content: You will need to try a good number of schemes, but try to find a point where you can split $$$f = \min(g, h)$$$ or similar. Then, divide and conquer will win.

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2207H1 - Bowser's Castle (Easy Version)
There are numerous ways to achieve the desired $$$O(n^2)$$$ query complexity. We will present one such approach due to 244mhq below.
Note that a min-max function $$$f$$$ can be represented by a tree of $$$\min$$$ and $$$\max$$$ calls. If we simplify all adjacent nodes that are $$$\max$$$ and $$$\min$$$, we will get a tree where the leaves are variables $$$x_i$$$ appearing in order and other nodes $$$\max$$$, $$$\min$$$ alternating along the paths to root. For instance, here is one such structure:
Further, due to the $$$0$$$-$$$1$$$ sorting lemma we may recover a boolean function $$$f$$$ where the $$$\max$$$ and $$$\min$$$ calls are replaced with ORs and ANDs, respectively. To figure out whether the root of the root of the tree is an OR or an AND, we may do the following: scan for the minimum $$$r$$$ such that $$$f(\overbrace{1, \ldots, 1}^{x_1, \ldots, x_r}, 0, \ldots, 0) = 1$$$ and the maximum $$$\ell$$$ such that $$$f(0, \dots, 0, \overbrace{1, \ldots, 1}^{x_\ell, \ldots, x_n}) = 1$$$. It is easy to show that the root node is an OR if and only if $$$r  \lt  \ell$$$, so we are able to find the root node.
It suffices to construct a recursive function that recovers $$$f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$$$ assuming the root node is an OR, since the AND case is symmetric up to negating inputs and outputs. To do this, let $$$m$$$ be the minimum index such that $$$f(\overbrace{1, \ldots, 1}^{x_1, \ldots, x_r}, 0, \ldots, 0) = 1$$$ and $$$k$$$ the minimum index such that $$$f(0, \ldots, 0, \overbrace{1, \ldots, 1}^{x_{m+1}, \ldots, x_k}, 0, \ldots, 0) = 1$$$. Note that $$$k$$$ must exist because we know that $$$f(0, \ldots, 0, \overbrace{1, \ldots, 1}^{x_{m+1}, \ldots, x_n}) = 1$$$ by our root node condition. In the example tree above, we would have $$$m = 2$$$ and $$$k = 3$$$.
The key observation is as follows. Greedily construct a sequence of inputs $$$x_1, \ldots, x_{k-1}$$$ where initially, all inputs are $$$1$$$, and notice that $$$f(x_1, \ldots, x_{k-1}, 0, \ldots, 0) = 1$$$. Iterate from $$$i = 1, \ldots, {k-1}$$$, and set $$$x_i = 0$$$ if $$$f(x_1, \ldots, x_{k-1}, 0, \ldots, 0) = 1$$$ still holds. If $$$z$$$ is the index of the largest $$$x_z = 1$$$ that remains, we claim that $$$f$$$ splits into $$$$$$f = \mathrm{OR}(f(x_1, \ldots, x_z), f(x_{z+1}, \ldots, x_n)).$$$$$$ This is easy to show by induction. So, we can recurse on the two pieces found by this split and obtain a query complexity of $$$2n^2 \leq 10^4$$$. This method can also be adjusted to $$$O(n \log n)$$$ by performing binary search to discover indices $$$r, \ell, m, k$$$ and simulating from both left and right endpoints.
